title: MobileNet
concept: MobileNet turns depthwise-separable convolutions from a local trick into a full architecture strategy, building CNNs for phones by separating spatial filtering from channel mixing across the network.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is SqueezeNet trying to achieve with its fire modules?

options.text: Competitive image understanding with far fewer parameters
Making every block wider no matter the hardware

exercises.explain: Right — MobileNet shares that efficiency goal, but uses a different core trick.

cards.title: A lighter engine for the same trip
cards.body: If SqueezeNet packed lighter luggage, MobileNet redesigned the engine itself. Instead of repeatedly paying for full standard convolutions, it asked: what if the whole network used a cheaper way to separate spatial pattern detection from channel mixing?

cards.title: What is MobileNet's main design move?
cards.body: MobileNet is an efficient CNN architecture that replaces many standard convolutions with depthwise-separable convolutions. Each block does a depthwise step to filter each channel spatially, then a pointwise 1x1 step to mix channels. Unit 101 introduced this idea as a cheap layer trick; MobileNet turns it into the backbone of the network.

cards.title: Two specialized stations instead of one giant machine
cards.body: A standard convolution is like one giant factory machine doing two jobs at once. MobileNet uses two smaller stations in sequence: first one checks spatial structure inside each channel, then the next remixes channels. Specialization makes the whole line cheaper to run.

cards.title: Why MobileNet maps so well to phones
cards.body: Because depthwise-separable convolutions use far fewer weights and operations than standard convolutions, MobileNet can run image tasks with much lower compute and memory demand. That makes it a natural fit for phones, embedded cameras, and other devices where real-time speed matters.

cards.title: MobileNet is not 'just one cheap layer'
cards.body: The important idea is architectural. Unit 101 showed why one depthwise-separable convolution is cheaper. MobileNet's real contribution is to organize an entire CNN around that cheaper primitive, so efficiency is built into the network from top to bottom.

cards.title: Standard block vs. MobileNet block
cards.body: Both produce useful features, but they spend compute differently.

left.text:
Spatial filtering + channel mixing
fused into one expensive step
higher compute cost

right.text:
Depthwise spatial step
then 1x1 channel mixing
much cheaper overall

exercises.prompt: What makes MobileNet more than just a reminder of Unit 101?

options.text: It uses depthwise-separable convolutions as a network-wide architectural strategy
It proves depthwise convolutions should never be followed by pointwise ones
It replaces feature maps with raw text tokens
It avoids all channel mixing entirely

exercises.explain: Correct — MobileNet makes the cheap block the default building block.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in a MobileNet-style block, the depthwise step handles spatial filtering while the pointwise step handles channel mixing.
exercises.explain: True — that is the core split that makes the block cheap.

exercises.sentence: MobileNet builds an efficient CNN by using depthwise-separable convolutions as a network-wide ___ strategy.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the key is the overall design, not a one-off trick.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best connects MobileNet back to Unit 101?

options.text: Unit 101 explained why depthwise-separable convolutions are cheap; MobileNet shows how to build a whole CNN around that idea
Unit 101 proved depthwise-separable convolutions are only useful in language models
MobileNet ignores channel mixing completely, unlike Unit 101

exercises.explain: Yes — MobileNet is the architecture-scale payoff of that earlier convolution lesson.

exercises.prompt: A team wants a CNN for a phone keyboard that recognizes handwritten characters in real time. Why would MobileNet often be a stronger candidate than a similarly accurate but standard-convolution-heavy model?

options.text: Because MobileNet builds the whole architecture around cheaper depthwise-separable blocks, making low-latency on-device inference more practical
Because MobileNet removes the need for channel mixing and therefore needs no 1x1 convolutions
Because phone hardware always runs large standard convolutions faster than cheap ones
Because depthwise-separable convolutions guarantee the best benchmark score on every task

exercises.explain: Exactly. MobileNet takes the cost-saving logic from Unit 101 and applies it across the full network, which is why it became a classic mobile architecture. Next: how do we decide when these efficient models are worth the tradeoff?
